_provenance.note: Discovery loop (Epic Y): the conjectures that graphstore.evaluate judged 'uncertain' (genuinely open, neither known nor false) refined into precise research directions by an IN-SESSION Claude Code sub-agent acting as an ML-theory research advisor. No API call (Max-plan in-session sub-agent), per the project methodology. Produced once in-session and committed; graphstore.discover validates this artifact DETERMINISTICALLY (schema + that every direction is anchored to a real conjecture the evaluation flagged uncertain). Closes the loop discover -> predict -> evaluate -> investigate.

directions.precise_statement: In Markov-chain/MDP settings, the contraction modulus of a Markovian operator is controlled by a likelihood-ratio/change-of-measure quantity: Dobrushin's ergodic coefficient and Doeblin's minorization condition (P(x,.) >= beta*nu(.)) yield geometric contraction with modulus (1-beta) in total variation, and in approximate dynamic programming the L2(mu)-contraction modulus of the projected Bellman operator is governed by concentrability coefficients C = sup ||d(P^pi rho)/d mu||_inf, i.e. a sup of Radon-Nikodym derivatives between the visitation distribution and the sampling distribution mu.
directions.whats_established: Doeblin's minorization gives geometric ergodicity with rate 1-beta (Meyn & Tweedie, Markov Chains and Stochastic Stability, Thm 16.0.2). Dobrushin's coefficient delta(P)=sup_{x,y}||P(x,.)-P(y,.)||_TV is submultiplicative and bounds TV-contraction. In ADP, Munos (2003, 'Error Bounds for Approximate Policy Iteration') and Munos & Szepesvari (2008, JMLR, 'Finite-Time Bounds for Fitted Value Iteration') show approximation-error propagation is governed by concentrability coefficients that are sup-norm Radon-Nikodym derivatives; Antos, Szepesvari & Munos (2008) extend this to single-sample-path fitted-Q. Bertsekas & Tsitsiklis (NDP, 1996) give the weighted-norm contraction of T^pi under the stationary-distribution-weighted L2 norm.
directions.open_question: Is there a sharp, non-trivial CHARACTERIZATION (not just an upper bound) of the L2(mu) contraction modulus of the projected Bellman operator Pi_mu T^pi in terms of a likelihood-ratio functional -- i.e. does the modulus equal (or is two-sided-bounded by) gamma times a measure of mismatch between mu and the discounted visitation distribution d^pi, rather than the loose sup-norm concentrability C that can be infinite even when the operator is contractive?
directions.next_step: Attempt to prove a two-sided bound gamma * f(mu, d^pi) <= ||Pi_mu T^pi||_{L2(mu) Lip} <= gamma * g(mu, d^pi), where f,g are explicit divergence-like functionals (e.g. chi-square divergence chi^2(d^pi||mu) or a spectral mismatch ratio lambda_max(Sigma_{d^pi}/Sigma_mu)) of the Radon-Nikodym derivative d(d^pi)/dmu; test tightness numerically on small finite MDPs (random chains, ~20 states) by comparing the empirically estimated operator Lipschitz modulus against the candidate functionals across a sweep of sampling distributions mu.

directions.precise_statement: The 'fixed point' core is type-correct not for the uniform-convergence statement itself but for the empirical-risk MINIMIZER: many M-estimators are defined by an estimating-equation/self-consistency fixed point, T_n(theta) = theta where T_n is the empirical map (e.g. the EM operator, the Newton/gradient map of a smooth empirical objective, or the proximal/contraction map of a regularized problem), and uniform convergence is what transfers properties of the population fixed point theta* (T(theta*)=theta*) to the empirical fixed point theta_n.
directions.whats_established: Self-consistency/fixed-point analyses of M-estimation are well developed: the EM operator's population fixed point and local geometric contraction are characterized by Balakrishnan, Wainwright & Yu (2017, Annals of Statistics, 'Statistical guarantees for the EM algorithm'); gradient-EM and general empirical-vs-population operator coupling appear there and in Wang, Gu, Ning & Liu (2015). Stochastic-approximation fixed-point theory (Robbins-Monro; Borkar, 'Stochastic Approximation: A Dynamical Systems Viewpoint') frames SGD/TD as tracking an ODE with an equilibrium. Uniform convergence (Vapnik-Chervonenkis; Bartlett-Mendelson Rademacher bounds, 2002) controls sup_f |R_n(f)-R(f)| but is itself a concentration statement, not a self-map.
directions.open_question: Can uniform-convergence rates be packaged as a quantitative perturbation bound on fixed points: given that the empirical operator T_n is uniformly close to the population operator T (sup ||T_n - T|| <= eps_n with high probability) and T is a local contraction with modulus kappa<1 near theta*, does ||theta_n - theta*|| <= eps_n/(1-kappa) hold with the SAME high probability, and what is the weakest uniform-convergence/stability condition on the operator class {T_theta} (as opposed to the loss class) that yields it?
directions.next_step: Prove a 'fixed-point uniform convergence' theorem: define the operator class F = {theta -> grad_theta ell(.,theta)} or the EM map, bound its Rademacher complexity / establish a uniform ||T_n - T||_inf <= eps_n bound, and combine with Banach perturbation (||theta_n-theta*|| <= eps_n/(1-kappa)) to get a finite-sample fixed-point error; instantiate concretely for Gaussian-mixture EM to recover the Balakrishnan-Wainwright-Yu rate purely from an operator-class uniform-convergence argument, then identify whether operator-class complexity can be strictly smaller than loss-class complexity.
